items.name: Cafézinho (espresso, padaria counter)
items.fact: Brazil produces one-third of the world's coffee yet the cafézinho at a padaria counter is the cheapest hot drink in any major economy. Brazilians drink 6 cups of coffee per day on average, more per capita than Italians.

items.name: Pão de Queijo (cheese bread, single, padaria)
items.fact: Pão de queijo originated in Minas Gerais in the 18th century when enslaved people made bread from tapioca starch left over from manioc processing. It is now Brazil's most exported food product.

items.name: Coxinha (fried chicken pastry, single, lanchonete)
items.fact: Coxinha is shaped like a chicken drumstick and named after the princess who had her illegitimate son's leg amputated and served to workers. Brazilians eat 5 billion coxinhas per year.

items.name: Litre of Petrol (regular, average Brazil)
items.fact: Brazil has the world's most developed flex-fuel car market. Over 80% of new cars sold can run on either petrol or ethanol, and Brazilians switch fuels based on which is cheaper that week.

items.name: Biscoito de Polvilho (cassava cracker, small pack)
items.fact: Polvilho crackers are made from fermented cassava starch and have a naturally hollow, light crunch. They are naturally gluten-free and became a global health food export before Brazilians knew what gluten-free meant.

items.name: Refrigerante Guaraná Antarctica (350ml can, supermarket)
items.fact: Guaraná Antarctica outsells Coca-Cola in several Brazilian states and is the only soft drink to topple Coke in a major market. It is made from the guaraná berry from the Amazon and contains nearly double the caffeine of coffee per gram.

items.name: Egg (single, standard, supermarket)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's fourth-largest egg producer and eggs are the most price-tracked food item by the government's IPCA inflation index. In 2021 egg prices spiked 60% in one year due to drought and corn price shocks, making them briefly unaffordable for lower-income Brazilians.

items.name: Água Mineral (500ml, Bonafont, supermarket)
items.fact: Brazil holds 12% of the world's surface freshwater yet sells billions of litres of bottled water annually. The Amazon carries more freshwater than the next 10 rivers combined but 40 million Brazilians still lack safe tap water.

items.name: Loaf of Bread (pão francês, single roll, padaria)
items.fact: Brazilians eat pão francês daily despite the name, as it was introduced by immigrants and adapted locally. The padaria, or neighbourhood bakery, is the social heart of every Brazilian street and is visited by 60% of adults daily.

items.name: Jornal (Folha de S.Paulo, daily print)
items.fact: Folha de S.Paulo is Brazil's largest newspaper and the most read in Latin America. Its price rise from 20 cents to R$7 in 35 years reflects Brazil's chronic inflation, as the country changed its currency five times between 1942 and 1994.

items.name: Cachaça shot (pinga, basic, bar counter)
items.fact: Cachaça is the world's third most consumed spirit after whisky and vodka. Brazil has 40,000 cachaça producers and spent 12 years fighting the US classification of it as rum, winning legal recognition as a distinct category in 2013.

items.name: Bauru Sandwich (traditional, lanchonete)
items.fact: The Bauru was invented in 1934 when a São Paulo student named Casemiro Pinto Neto asked for an improvised sandwich and it became a national standard. It is the only Brazilian food protected by a São Paulo state law specifying its exact ingredients.

items.name: Arroz (rice, 1kg, Tio João, supermarket)
items.fact: Rice and beans is Brazil's national dish and is eaten daily by 70% of the population. In 2020 rice prices doubled due to export demand from Asia and the real's depreciation, triggering the first rice protests since the 1980s hyperinflation era.

items.name: Litre of Ethanol (sugar cane fuel, pump average)
items.fact: Brazil launched the world's first national biofuel programme in 1975 after the oil crisis. Its sugar-cane ethanol is the most efficient biofuel ever produced and Brazil's flex-fuel revolution is studied by every country attempting energy transition.

items.name: Feijão (black beans, 1kg, supermarket)
items.fact: Feijão preto, black beans, is Brazil's most consumed staple and its price is the most politically sensitive food metric in the country. When beans doubled in price in 2020, President Bolsonaro suggested Brazilians eat less because it causes flatulence.

items.name: Chocolate (Bis, single bar, supermarket)
items.fact: Bis chocolate wafers are Brazil's best-selling individual confectionery item with 1.5 billion units sold annually. Lacta, the Brazilian confectionery giant, was acquired by Kraft in 2012 but Bis has maintained its exact recipe and packaging since 1961.

items.name: Ônibus (city bus, single fare, São Paulo)
items.fact: In 2013 a São Paulo bus fare hike of 20 centavos triggered the Vinagre Protests, the largest street demonstrations in Brazil since the 1992 impeachment of Collor. The government reversed the hike within days.

items.name: Pipoca (popcorn, street vendor, paper bag)
items.fact: Pipoca vendors are a fixture outside every Brazilian church, stadium and school. Brazil is the world's third-largest popcorn market and the Pipoqueiro, the popcorn vendor, is a beloved urban character that appears in Brazilian literature and music.

items.name: Paçoca (peanut candy, single piece, vendor)
items.fact: Paçoca is a crumbly peanut sweet that is Brazil's most consumed regional candy and is inseparable from Festa Junina celebrations in June. Brazil is the world's third-largest peanut producer and exports paçoca to Portuguese-speaking communities globally.

items.name: Cerveja (beer, 350ml lata, boteco counter)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's third-largest beer market. Skol and Brahma, both owned by AB InBev since the 1999 merger of Ambev, together hold 65% of the market. Brazil's boteco bar culture is UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.

items.name: Cartão de Transporte (Bilhete Único, Rio Metro, single)
items.fact: Rio's metro system covers only 41km for a city of 7 million and was consistently underfunded until 2016 Olympic preparations forced an expansion. São Paulo's metro carries 5 million passengers daily on 100km of lines, still less than London at a third of the cost.

items.name: Banana (single, prata variety, feira)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's fifth-largest banana producer but almost no Brazilian bananas are exported because domestic demand consumes the entire crop. The prata variety, grown mainly in Minas Gerais, has a flavour Brazilians insist cannot be replicated outside the country.

items.name: Farinha de Mandioca (cassava flour, 500g, supermarket)
items.fact: Manioc flour is sprinkled on virtually every Brazilian dish from feijoada to fried eggs and has been the country's dietary staple since before Portuguese arrival. Brazil produces 26% of the world's cassava, more than any other country.

items.name: Açaí (300ml cup, street vendor, com granola)
items.fact: Açaí was a staple food for Amazonian communities for centuries before becoming a global superfood in the 2000s. Brazil's açaí export value rose from near zero to R$3 billion between 2010 and 2023 as demand from the US and China exploded.

items.name: Bala (hard candy, single, loja 1.99)
items.fact: Brazilians exchange balas as informal change at cashiers when coins run short, a practice so common that Procon consumer protection agencies have ruled it technically illegal but universally tolerated.

items.name: Caipirinha (classic, bar, standard)
items.fact: Caipirinha is Brazil's national cocktail and is made with cachaça, lime and sugar. It was created during the 1918 Spanish flu as a home remedy before becoming a leisure drink. It is now the most ordered Brazilian drink in 45 countries.

items.name: Cinema Ticket (Cinemark / UCI, standard 2D)
items.fact: Brazil has the largest cinema market in Latin America with 3,500 screens. The half-price ticket on Wednesdays is a cultural institution that fills multiplexes across the country and has been maintained for 30 years despite inflation.

items.name: Corte de Cabelo (men's, barbearia)
items.fact: Brazil has the world's largest barbershop market outside the US with over 600,000 registered establishments. The Brazilian barbearia revival of the 2010s turned a simple haircut into a lifestyle experience with beards, whisky and vinyl records.

items.name: Feijoada Completa (restaurant, Friday special, per person)
items.fact: Feijoada is Brazil's national dish and is eaten every Friday at restaurants across the country. It was once food of enslaved people who used the scraps of pork the plantation owners didn't want. Today it is Brazil's most ceremonial meal.

items.name: Netflix Assinatura (monthly, standard, Brazil)
items.fact: Brazil is Netflix's second-largest market outside the US by subscriber count. Netflix invested over R$500 million in Brazilian original content, making Brazil one of the largest producers of Netflix originals after the US and UK.

items.name: Academia Smart Fit (monthly, budget gym, Brazil)
items.fact: Smart Fit launched in 2009 and disrupted the gym market with R$59 monthly fees, becoming Latin America's largest gym chain within 10 years. Brazil has the second-largest gym market in the world after the US with 35,000 academias.

items.name: Pastel (fried pastry with cheese, feira)
items.fact: Pastel was adapted from Chinese spring rolls by Japanese-Brazilian immigrants in São Paulo's Liberdade district. The Saturday feira market pastel is a ritual for millions of Paulistas and the Pastelaria Albano in Liberdade has operated since 1941.

items.name: Tapioca (filled crepe, street vendor)
items.fact: Tapioca is made from manioc starch and has been a Northeast Brazilian staple for 500 years. It became a health food trend in the 2010s and now appears in French, Japanese and American restaurants as a premium ingredient.

items.name: Spotify Assinatura (monthly, Brazil)
items.fact: Brazil is Spotify's largest market in Latin America and its most diverse by genre. Brazilian funk, sertanejo and pagode each have larger Spotify listener bases than entire national music industries of most European countries.

items.name: Churrasco (BBQ at restaurante, per 100g, prime cut)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's largest beef exporter and the churrascaria restaurant format with unlimited meat on skewers brought to the table is one of Brazil's most successful culinary exports, now operating in 50 countries.

items.name: Pizza (large, delivery, Domino's Brazil)
items.fact: São Paulo has the largest pizza market outside New York City with 8,000 pizzerias. Brazilians invented sweet pizza toppings including chocolate and banana, a concept that scandalises Italian chefs but accounts for 30% of São Paulo orders.

items.name: Bermuda Havaianas (flip flops, standard pair)
items.fact: Havaianas launched in 1962 and sold exclusively to low-income workers until a 1994 rebranding as a fashion item. Today they sell 250 million pairs annually in 100 countries and the factory in Campina Grande works 24 hours to meet global demand.

items.name: Gasolina Aditivada (6 litros premium, posto)
items.fact: Petrobras controls 85% of Brazil's fuel distribution and its pricing policy is among the most politically contested economic decisions in the country. Every presidential election since 2006 has included promises to lower fuel prices as a central pledge.

items.name: Pack of Marlboro (20 cigarettes)
items.fact: Brazil was the first country in the world to ban all cigarette advertising in 2000. Smoking rates fell from 35% to 12% of adults between 1990 and 2023, one of the steepest declines ever recorded in a large country.

items.name: Curso Online (Duolingo Plus, monthly, Brazil)
items.fact: Brazil is Duolingo's largest market globally and the only country where English learning app usage exceeds daily social media time in the education category. Learning English for career advancement is cited as the primary financial aspiration of 60% of working-class Brazilians.

items.name: Brigadeiro (chocolate truffle, confeitaria, single)
items.fact: Brigadeiro was created in the 1940s in honour of Brigadier Eduardo Gomes and is Brazil's most beloved dessert. Gourmet brigadeiros at R$12 each are a São Paulo confectionery business that grew from birthday parties into a R$2 billion industry.

items.name: Farmácia (Dorflex paracetamol + dipyrone combo, 36 tabs)
items.fact: Dipyrone (metamizole) is banned in many countries but is Brazil's most widely used pain medication. Brazilians consume 4 billion dipyrone tablets annually, making it the country's most-sold pharmaceutical product by volume.

items.name: Conta de Luz (residential electricity bill, monthly avg)
items.fact: Brazil gets 80% of its electricity from hydroelectric dams, one of the cleanest grids in the world. But when a drought hits the Paraná basin, electricity prices spike 40% overnight and Brazil has an emergency 'energy flag' system that adds surcharges visible on every bill.

items.name: Caldo de Cana (sugar cane juice, 500ml, vendor)
items.fact: Freshly pressed caldo de cana is Brazil's most distinctive street drink and is sold from mechanical presses in every city. Brazil processes 700 million tonnes of sugar cane annually, more than any other country by a factor of three.

items.name: Ingresso (Flamengo vs Fluminense, Maracanã, geral)
items.fact: Flamengo is the world's most followed football club by social media with 45 million followers. The Rio derby at the Maracanã attracts 70,000 fans and a Fla-Flu match is considered Brazil's most intense sporting rivalry, described by journalists since the 1940s.

items.name: Combo McDonald's (McOferta, standard, Brazil)
items.fact: McDonald's Brazil sells the Cheddar McMelt, a burger with processed cheddar and caramelised onions, exclusive to Brazil and the most ordered item in the country. It was created in Brazil in 1985 and has never been offered on any other national menu.

items.name: Aluguel (1-bedroom, monthly, outskirts São Paulo)
items.fact: São Paulo's outer-ring 1-bedroom rents doubled between 2020 and 2023 as the pandemic pushed workers further from the centre. Brazil has a formal housing deficit of 8 million units, more than the entire housing stock of Portugal.

items.name: Plano de Saúde (basic health plan, monthly, individual)
items.fact: Brazil has both a universal public health system (SUS) and a private insurance market. 50 million Brazilians pay for private plans because SUS wait times average 14 months for specialist appointments. The private plan market is the largest in Latin America.

items.name: Máscara de Carnaval (costume mask, loja de fantasias)
items.fact: Rio Carnival generates R$6 billion in economic activity over 6 days. A luxury Sambódromo fantasy costume can cost R$5,000 but basic street carnival masks are sold for R$5 on every corner, making it the world's most economically stratified festival.

items.name: Livro (Paulo Coelho paperback, Livraria Cultura)
items.fact: Paulo Coelho's The Alchemist is the most translated book by a living author with 85 translations. Brazil has the world's fifth-largest book market but the lowest reading rate among literate adults, averaging 4.96 books per year versus France's 21.

items.name: iPhone 16 (standard, 128GB, Brazil retail)
items.fact: iPhones in Brazil are the most expensive in the world due to a 108% tax on imported electronics. Apple built a factory in Jundiaí in 2012 specifically to qualify for a local manufacturing tax exemption, but prices remain 2 to 3 times the US equivalent.

items.name: Passagem Aérea (São Paulo to Rio, economy, avg)
items.fact: The São Paulo to Rio air bridge was the world's busiest route for 30 years, operating from Santos Dumont airport with no luggage allowed and boarding without a ticket on some carriers. Azul and Gol disrupted Varig's monopoly and reduced fares by 60% between 2000 and 2010.

items.name: Notebook (laptop, Positivo, entry 8GB RAM)
items.fact: Positivo is Brazil's only domestic laptop brand and has survived solely due to government procurement contracts. Brazil's electronics tax regime (IPI) adds 40% to the cost of imported laptops, making local assembly competitive despite lower volumes.

items.name: Tênis Nike Air Max (entry, standard colourway, Brazil)
items.fact: Nike trainers in Brazil cost 3 times the US price due to import taxes and are a major status symbol in favelas where ownership represents weeks of minimum wage earnings. Robbery for trainers is a documented crime category in São Paulo and Rio.

items.name: Plano Celular (monthly smartphone plan, 30GB, Vivo)
items.fact: Brazil's mobile market was privatised in 1998 and four operators fought for dominance in the most competitive telecoms market in Latin America. Prices fell 50% between 2015 and 2023, and Brazil now has the cheapest mobile data in Latin America per gigabyte.

items.name: Aluguel (2-bedroom, monthly, Zona Sul São Paulo)
items.fact: São Paulo's Zona Sul rents tripled between 2010 and 2015 during Brazil's commodity boom. The subsequent crash stabilised prices until 2021 when a new wave of remote workers from tech companies pushed rents to record levels again.

items.name: Academia Premium (annual, CrossFit / premium, São Paulo)
items.fact: CrossFit gyms proliferated in São Paulo between 2012 and 2018 faster than any city outside the US. Brazil has 7,000 CrossFit boxes, more than the US per capita, driven by a fitness culture that treats the gym as a social environment.

items.name: Seguro Auto (annual, comprehensive, Fiat Strada)
items.fact: Brazil has one of the world's highest vehicle theft rates and car insurance premiums reflect it. São Paulo alone has 100,000 vehicle thefts per year, making comprehensive insurance a financial necessity rather than a choice for car owners.

items.name: Cesta Básica (basic food basket, monthly, São Paulo)
items.fact: The cesta básica, a standardised basket of 13 essential foods, is tracked monthly by DIEESE and compared to the minimum wage. In 2022 the basket cost 68% of the minimum wage, the highest proportion since tracking began in 1959.

items.name: Mensalidade Escolar (private school, monthly, medium quality)
items.fact: Private school fees in Brazil rose 50x since 1990. Brazil's public schools have a 50% dropout rate by 9th grade but private school students have a 95% university entrance rate, creating education as the primary driver of income inequality.

items.name: Ingresso Lollapalooza (1-day, São Paulo)
items.fact: Lollapalooza Brasil launched in 2012 and now draws 100,000 people per day over 3 days. São Paulo has overtaken Buenos Aires as Latin America's music capital and hosts more major international tours than any other city in the Southern Hemisphere.

items.name: Smartphone Samsung Galaxy A55 (mid-range, Brazil)
items.fact: Samsung dominates Brazil's smartphone market with 40% share because it manufactures in Manaus under the Zona Franca de Manaus free trade zone, lowering its price 30% versus imported competitors. The Manaus free zone was created in 1967 to encourage Amazon urbanisation.

items.name: Passagem SP-Recife (overnight bus, conventional)
items.fact: Brazil has the world's largest intercity bus network with 900 companies and 15 billion passengers per year. The São Paulo to Recife route takes 42 hours and is the longest regularly operated bus route in the world.

items.name: Consulta Médica Privada (specialist, average)
items.fact: A private specialist consultation in São Paulo costs R$700 versus a 14-month SUS wait for the same appointment at no cost. This gap drives Brazil's R$500 billion private healthcare market, the largest in the developing world.

items.name: Carro Usado (Fiat Uno, 2010, good condition)
items.fact: Used car prices in Brazil peaked in 2021 when a semiconductor shortage halted new car production. A 10-year-old Fiat Uno briefly cost the same as a new one because new cars weren't available. Prices normalised after 2023 as supply chains recovered.

items.name: Solar Panels (residential kit, 3kW, installed avg)
items.fact: Brazil added 15GW of rooftop solar capacity in 5 years after a 2012 regulatory change allowed net metering. Solar payback periods fell from 12 years to 4.5 years and Brazil now has the world's fastest-growing residential solar market outside China.

items.name: Festa de Casamento (wedding, standard, per person)
items.fact: The average Brazilian wedding costs R$57,000 and the wedding market generates R$22 billion annually. Brazil has a unique wedding industry feature called bem-casados, almond sweets in tulle bags given to every guest as a take-home gift from the couple.

items.name: Aluguel Temporada Carnaval (Ipanema, per night, modest flat)
items.fact: Ipanema Carnival rentals for a modest flat average R$2,000 per night during the pre-Carnival weekend. The Carnival weekend generates R$6 billion for Rio in 6 days, with 1.5 million foreign tourists, the densest tourist concentration per square kilometre of any annual event.

items.name: Taxa de Matrícula (federal university, symbolic, Brazil)
items.fact: Federal universities in Brazil are free for all Brazilian citizens and admission is based purely on ENEM exam scores. This system democratised higher education but created a paradox where some of the world's best-ranked universities charge nothing.

items.name: Plano de Saúde Premium (monthly, individual, Bradesco)
items.fact: Bradesco Saúde is Brazil's largest private health insurer with 25 million members. Its premium plan at R$3,000 per month covers any hospital in Brazil including Albert Einstein in São Paulo, consistently ranked Latin America's best hospital.

items.name: PlayStation 5 (disc edition, Brazil retail)
items.fact: PlayStation 5 in Brazil costs R$5,000 versus USD 499 in the US, roughly 3.5x the US price in purchasing power terms. Brazil's electronics tax of 60-108% is the world's highest on consumer tech and is constitutionally protected as part of the Manaus free trade zone.

items.name: Gold (10 grams, 24K, joyeria, São Paulo)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's third-largest gold producer with major mines in Pará and Minas Gerais. Illegal gold mining in the Amazon, known as garimpo, devastated Yanomami territory and became an international human rights crisis between 2019 and 2023.

items.name: Tênis Nike Air Jordan 1 (Brazil retail, standard)
items.fact: Brazil has the world's largest Air Jordan resale market outside North America and Japan. Limited Jordans released in Brazil sell out in minutes and resell for 5x retail, creating a grey market managed entirely through Instagram and WhatsApp groups.

items.name: Carro Novo (Fiat Argo, base, 0km, Brazil)
items.fact: Brazil is the world's seventh-largest car market and Fiat is its best-selling brand. The real's depreciation pushed new car prices in R$ up 50% between 2018 and 2023 while average wages grew only 20%, making a new car an increasingly aspirational rather than practical purchase.

items.name: Hotel (3-star, Rio de Janeiro, standard room, Ipanema)
items.fact: Ipanema hotel rates hit record highs during the 2016 Rio Olympics before crashing 40% within a year as the post-Games hotel glut flooded supply. By 2025 rates had recovered to near-Olympic levels driven by domestic tourism from Brazil's growing middle class.

items.name: iPhone 16 Pro Max (256GB, Brazil retail)
items.fact: At R$13,499, Brazil's iPhone Pro Max is the most expensive in the world relative to local wages. A Brazilian minimum wage earner would need 9 months of gross salary to buy one, versus 2 weeks for a US minimum wage worker.

items.name: Volkswagen Polo (base, 0km, Brazil)
items.fact: Volkswagen has manufactured in Brazil since 1953 and the original Beetle, locally called Fusca, ran in production until 1996, longer than anywhere else in the world. The Brazilian-made Gol was the best-selling car in Latin America for 27 consecutive years.

items.name: Gold (troy ounce, BRL, São Paulo spot)
items.fact: Gold in BRL terms is amplified by the real's depreciation, rising 55x since 1990 versus 9x in USD terms. Brazilian investors treat gold as their primary inflation hedge, making the São Paulo gold spot price a closely watched economic indicator.

items.name: Apartamento Médio (2-bedroom, purchase, Zona Sul SP, per unit)
items.fact: São Paulo's Zona Sul apartment prices rose 3x between 2005 and 2015 during the Brazilian commodity boom. After a 5-year plateau they began rising again in 2020 as interest rates fell to record lows before rising sharply after 2022.

items.name: Fiat Toro (pickup truck, base, Brazil)
items.fact: The Fiat Toro is Brazil's best-selling pickup truck and was designed entirely in Brazil for Brazilian roads, the first Brazilian-designed vehicle to reach international sales. Pickup trucks became Brazil's best-selling vehicle category in 2021, overtaking sedans for the first time.

items.name: Rolex Submariner (new retail, São Paulo)
items.fact: Rolex in Brazil costs 4 times the US retail price because of an 80% import tax on luxury watches. São Paulo's Oscar Freire street is Latin America's most expensive retail strip and its Rolex ADs have waitlists exceeding 5 years.

items.name: Mensalidade Faculdade (private university, annual, medicine)
items.fact: Private medical school in Brazil costs R$200,000 per year and only accepts students who failed ENEM for federal medicine. Brazilian doctors are among the world's highest-earning professionals, earning 20x the minimum wage, making the investment rational despite the cost.

items.name: Casa em Condomínio (gated community house, São Paulo outskirts)
items.fact: Brazil has more gated communities per capita than any country in the world. Fear of crime drove the condomínio fechado movement from the 1980s onward and São Paulo has entire cities within cities, with private police, schools and hospitals inaccessible to non-residents.

items.name: Tesla Model 3 (standard range, Brazil retail)
items.fact: Tesla Model 3 in Brazil costs R$280,000 versus USD 38,000 in the US, nearly 8x the US price at purchasing power parity. Electric vehicles face the same import taxes as all electronics, making Brazil's EV transition the slowest of any large economy.

items.name: Apartamento Lux (Jardins, São Paulo, 3-bed, purchase)
items.fact: São Paulo's Jardins neighbourhood has the highest apartment prices in Latin America. A 3-bedroom there costs more per square metre than Miami Beach, despite average Brazilian wages being 8 times lower than American wages.

items.name: Louis Vuitton Neverfull MM (São Paulo retail)
items.fact: Louis Vuitton Brazil prices are among the highest in the world due to 130% luxury import taxes. Brazilian tourists in Paris and Miami carry empty suitcases specifically to fill with luxury goods for personal use under the import exemption threshold.

items.name: Cirurgia Plástica (rhinoplasty, top SP surgeon)
items.fact: Brazil performs more plastic surgeries per capita than any country except South Korea, with 1.5 million procedures annually. Brazilian plastic surgery is world-renowned and the country trains more cosmetic surgeons than Europe and North America combined.

items.name: Passagem SP-Paris (economy, TAM/LATAM, avg)
items.fact: LATAM Airlines, formed from the 2012 merger of LAN and TAM, is Latin America's largest airline and carries more passengers across the Atlantic than any European airline on South American routes. Post-COVID inflation pushed SP-Paris fares to 10-year highs.

items.name: Carnaval Fantasia (luxury Sambódromo costume, full)
items.fact: A luxury Sambódromo costume for a Série A escola costs R$18,000 and is worn for exactly 80 minutes. The costumes weigh up to 40kg and are built by artisans who work on them for 6 months. After the parade they are destroyed to prevent copying.

items.name: Fazenda (small farm, 50 hectares, interior SP, purchase)
items.fact: Brazilian farmland is the world's fastest-appreciating commodity, rising 30% in real terms annually between 2020 and 2023. Brazil became the world's largest soybean exporter in 2012 and agricultural land in the Cerrado now trades at prices comparable to suburban European real estate.

items.name: Porsche 911 (base Carrera, Brazil MSRP)
items.fact: A Porsche 911 costs R$1.8 million in Brazil versus R$640,000 equivalent in Germany. Brazil applies a 120% import tax on luxury cars plus IPVA annual vehicle tax, making Brazil one of the world's most expensive places to own a premium European car.

items.name: Hospedagem Pantanal (5-day luxury wildlife lodge, per person)
items.fact: The Pantanal is the world's largest tropical wetland and has the highest concentration of jaguars in the world. Luxury eco-lodges that offer jaguar tracking charge R$18,000 for 5 days and are booked 18 months in advance by international wildlife photographers.

items.name: Apartamento Ipanema (2-bedroom, purchase, per unit)
items.fact: An Ipanema 2-bedroom apartment costs R$3.5 million, making the beachfront neighbourhood one of the few places in the developing world where prices are comparable to Manhattan or Mayfair. The Girl from Ipanema was inspired by Heloísa Pinheiro, who walked past a café to the same beach every day.

items.name: Helicóptero Charter (São Paulo city, 1 hour)
items.fact: São Paulo has the world's largest urban helicopter fleet with 2,000 registered aircraft. São Paulo's traffic is so severe that the city has more helicopter operations per day than New York, London and Los Angeles combined, used by executives to avoid ground traffic.

items.name: Cirurgia Bariátrica (gastric bypass, private hospital SP)
items.fact: Brazil performs 60,000 bariatric surgeries per year through both the SUS public system and private hospitals. The procedure is covered by SUS for eligible patients, making Brazil the only country where low-income citizens can receive free weight loss surgery.

items.name: Mega-Sena Prêmio Máximo (jackpot draw, single ticket cost)
items.fact: Mega-Sena is Brazil's national lottery and its jackpots regularly exceed R$200 million. Over 100 million Brazilians buy a ticket each draw and betting houses are Brazil's fastest-growing retail format, outnumbering pharmacies in some states.

items.name: Mercedes-Benz GLE (SUV, base, Brazil MSRP)
items.fact: Mercedes-Benz Brazil assembles trucks and buses in São Bernardo do Campo but imports all passenger cars, triggering the luxury car tax. The GLE at R$1.4 million costs more than a US house, but still sells 2,000 units per year to Brazil's 250,000 millionaires.

items.name: Curso MBA (FGV, top Brazilian school, full programme)
items.fact: FGV and Insper are ranked among Latin America's top business schools and their MBAs cost R$140,000. Graduate from either with a specialisation in agribusiness and average starting salaries reach R$25,000 per month, the fastest return on educational investment in the country.

items.name: Mega-fazenda (large farm, 500 hectares, Mato Grosso)
items.fact: Mato Grosso farmland prices rose 10x in 15 years as soy demand from China transformed the Cerrado into the world's most productive agricultural frontier. Brazil planted soy on an area the size of France and the fazendeiro became one of the 21st century's most powerful economic figures.

items.name: Iate Lancha (speedboat, 28ft, Intermarine, new)
items.fact: Brazil has the world's third-largest recreational boat market after the US and Europe. The Guarujá and Angra dos Reis coastlines have more private speedboats per kilometre of shoreline than Monaco, concentrated among São Paulo's industrial and agribusiness elite.
